 {"id":"bv-1x6o","title":"History: Timeline Visualization Panel","description":"# History: Timeline Visualization Panel

## Problem Statement
The History view has rich timestamp data (lifecycle events, commits) but presents it as a flat list. Users can't visualize "when things happened" at a glance. A visual timeline would make patterns immediately apparent.

## What Data We Have
\`\`\`go
type BeadHistory struct {
    Events    []BeadEvent        // created, claimed, closed, modified
    Milestones BeadMilestones    // Quick access to key events
    Commits   []CorrelatedCommit // Each has Timestamp
    CycleTime *CycleTime         // claim_to_close, create_to_close
}
\`\`\`

## Design

### Timeline Panel (appears in wide layout)
\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│ TIMELINE: bv-123                │
│ ────────────────────────────────│
│                                 │
│  Jan 10 ┃ ○ Created             │
│         ┃ │                     │
│  Jan 12 ┃ ● Claimed by Alice    │
│         ┃ │                     │
│  Jan 13 ┃ ├─ a1b2c3d 95%        │
│         ┃ │   Fix auth bug      │
│         ┃ │                     │
│  Jan 14 ┃ ├─ b2c3d4e 87%        │
│         ┃ │   Add session mgmt  │
│         ┃ │                     │
│  Jan 15 ┃ ├─ c3d4e5f 65%        │
│         ┃ │   Refactor          │
│         ┃ │                     │
│  Today  ┃ ✓ Closed              │
│         ┃                       │
│ ─────────────────────────────── │
│ Cycle: 5d 4h (create→close)     │
│ Commits: 3 (avg 87% confidence) │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
\`\`\`

### Visual Elements

#### Event Markers
- ○ Created (open circle)
- ● Claimed/In Progress (filled circle)
- ✓ Closed (checkmark)
- ↻ Reopened (cycle arrow)
- ◆ Modified (diamond)

#### Commit Markers
- ├─ Commit branch from timeline
- Colored by confidence: green (≥80%), yellow (50-79%), gray (<50%)
- Show short SHA and abbreviated message

#### Time Scale
- Smart date formatting: "2h ago", "Yesterday", "Jan 15", etc.
- Compress gaps when there's no activity
- Show relative timestamps on left axis

### Compact Timeline (for bead list)
When selecting a bead in narrow view, show mini-timeline:
\`\`\`
bv-123: Auth refactor
○──●──├──├──├──✓  5d cycle, 3 commits
   Jan 10 ──────────────────── Jan 15
\`\`\`

### Implementation

\`\`\`go
type TimelineEntry struct {
    Timestamp time.Time
    Type      string  // "event" or "commit"
    Label     string  // Event type or commit SHA
    Detail    string  // Full message or event detail
    Color     lipgloss.TerminalColor
}

func (h *HistoryModel) buildTimeline(hist BeadHistory) []TimelineEntry {
    var entries []TimelineEntry
    
    // Add lifecycle events
    for _, event := range hist.Events {
        entries = append(entries, TimelineEntry{
            Timestamp: event.Timestamp,
            Type:      "event",
            Label:     string(event.EventType),
            Color:     h.colorForEvent(event.EventType),
        })
    }
    
    // Add commits
    for _, commit := range hist.Commits {
        entries = append(entries, TimelineEntry{
            Timestamp: commit.Timestamp,
            Type:      "commit",
            Label:     commit.ShortSHA,
            Detail:    commit.Message,
            Color:     h.colorForConfidence(commit.Confidence),
        })
    }
    
    // Sort chronologically
    sort.Slice(entries, func(i, j int) bool {
        return entries[i].Timestamp.Before(entries[j].Timestamp)
    })
    
    return entries
}
\`\`\`

### Rendering with Box Drawing
Use Unicode box-drawing characters for clean vertical timeline:
- │ Vertical line
- ├─ Branch to commit
- ┃ Bold vertical for emphasis
- ○●✓ Event markers

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Timeline panel renders in wide layout (> 150 cols)
- [ ] Shows lifecycle events with distinct markers
- [ ] Shows commits with confidence coloring
- [ ] Timestamps use smart relative formatting
- [ ] Cycle time summary at bottom
- [ ] Compact timeline available for narrow views
- [ ] Scrollable if many entries

## Visual Polish
- Smooth color gradients for confidence
- Consistent spacing between entries
- Elegant time axis labeling
- Proper alignment of all elements","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-17T15:15:01.217542-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T21:52:02.631553-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T21:52:02.631553-05:00","close_reason":"Timeline panel implemented with tests","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bv-1x6o","depends_on_id":"bv-xrfh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-17T15:18:08.207672-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"bv-2fpk","title":"Tree: Implement navigation and keyboard handling","description":"## Purpose
Enable users to navigate the tree structure intuitively using keyboard shortcuts, with expand/collapse functionality.

## Navigation Model

### The flatList Concept
Trees are 2D but keyboard navigation is 1D. We solve this by maintaining a **flatList** - a flattened array of currently visible nodes.

```
Visual Tree:              flatList index:
▾ EPIC-001                [0]
  ├─ FEAT-002             [1]
  ├─ ▸ FEAT-003           [2] (collapsed - children hidden)
  └─ TASK-004             [3]
▸ EPIC-005                [4] (collapsed)
```

When FEAT-003 is expanded:
```
Visual Tree:              flatList index:
▾ EPIC-001                [0]
  ├─ FEAT-002             [1]
  ├─ ▾ FEAT-003           [2]
  │   ├─ TASK-006         [3] (newly visible)
  │   └─ TASK-007         [4] (newly visible)
  └─ TASK-004             [5]
▸ EPIC-005                [6]
```

### RebuildFlatList Algorithm
```go
func (t *TreeModel) RebuildFlatList() {
    t.flatList = t.flatList[:0] // Reset
    for _, root := range t.roots {
        t.appendVisible(root)
    }
}

func (t *TreeModel) appendVisible(node *TreeNode) {
    t.flatList = append(t.flatList, node)
    if node.Expanded {
        for _, child := range node.Children {
            t.appendVisible(child)
        }
    }
}
```

## Keyboard Mappings

### Navigation
| Key | Action | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| `j` / `↓` | Move cursor down | Wraps at bottom (optional) |
| `k` / `↑` | Move cursor up | Wraps at top (optional) |
| `gg` | Jump to first node | Vim-style |
| `G` | Jump to last node | |
| `Ctrl+D` | Page down | Half-viewport scroll |
| `Ctrl+U` | Page up | |

### Expand/Collapse
| Key | Action | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| `Enter` / `Space` | Toggle expand/collapse | On leaf node: no-op or show detail |
| `→` / `l` | Expand current | If already expanded: move to first child |
| `←` / `h` | Collapse current | If already collapsed: jump to parent |
| `o` | Expand all descendants | Recursive expand |
| `O` | Collapse all | Reset to roots-only |
| `zo` | Expand current (vim-style) | Alternative |
| `zc` | Collapse current | Alternative |
| `zM` | Collapse all | Alternative |
| `zR` | Expand all | Alternative |

### Exit
| Key | Action | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| `E` | Exit tree view | Return to list |
| `Esc` | Exit tree view | Alternative |
| `q` | Exit tree OR quit app | Context-dependent |

## Implementation Details

### MoveDown() / MoveUp()
```go
func (t *TreeModel) MoveDown() {
    if t.cursor < len(t.flatList)-1 {
        t.cursor++
        t.ensureVisible()
    }
}

func (t *TreeModel) ensureVisible() {
    // Scroll viewport if cursor is outside visible range
    // Similar to list.Model behavior
}
```

### ToggleExpand()
```go
func (t *TreeModel) ToggleExpand() {
    if t.cursor >= len(t.flatList) {
        return
    }
    node := t.flatList[t.cursor]
    if len(node.Children) == 0 {
        return // Leaf node - nothing to toggle
    }
    node.Expanded = !node.Expanded
    t.RebuildFlatList()
    // Cursor may need adjustment if collapsing
}
```

### JumpToParent()
```go
func (t *TreeModel) JumpToParent() {
    if t.cursor >= len(t.flatList) {
        return
    }
    node := t.flatList[t.cursor]
    if node.Parent == nil {
        return // Already at root
    }
    // Find parent in flatList
    for i, n := range t.flatList {
        if n == node.Parent {
            t.cursor = i
            t.ensureVisible()
            return
        }
    }
}
```

## State Management

### Preserving Cursor on Rebuild
When tree is rebuilt (filter change, data reload):
1. Remember current issue ID
2. Rebuild tree
3. Find same issue ID in new flatList
4. Set cursor to that index (or 0 if not found)

### Preserving Expand State
Store expanded IDs in a set:
```go
expandedIDs map[string]bool
```
On rebuild, restore expanded state from this set.

- [ ] → expands or moves to first child
- [ ] gg/G jump to top/bottom
- [ ] Expand/collapse rebuilds flatList correctly
- [ ] Cursor preserved across filter changes

 {"id":"bv-2h40","title":"Phase 3: Pre-computed Views - Build All View Data in Background","description":"# Phase 3: Pre-computed Views

## Overview

After Phase 2, the background worker loads data and runs analysis. But the UI still transforms this data into renderable structures (list items, board columns, tree nodes). This transformation can take 50-100ms.

Phase 3 moves view pre-computation to the background, so rendering becomes O(visible items) instead of O(all items).

## Why This Phase

Phase 2 made I/O and analysis non-blocking. But we still have:
- ListItems construction: iterating all issues, applying filters, building delegate items
- BoardState construction: grouping by status, sorting within columns
- TreeNodes construction: building hierarchical structure from parent-child links
- GraphLayout construction: node positioning, edge routing
- InsightsData construction: extracting top-N for each metric

By pre-computing all of this in the background, the View() method becomes trivial: just render what's already computed.

## The Goal

```go
// BEFORE (current): View() does work
func (m Model) View() string {
    items := m.buildListItems(m.issues)  // O(n) - 50ms for 1000 issues
    return m.renderItems(items)           // O(visible) - fast
}

// AFTER (Phase 3): View() just renders
func (m Model) View() string {
    return m.renderItems(m.snapshot.ListItems) // O(visible) - fast
}
```

## Pre-computed Structures

### 1. ListItems
```go
type ListItemsSnapshot struct {
    Items       []list.Item    // Ready for bubbles/list
    FilteredIDs []string       // Which issues are currently visible
    SortMode    SortMode       // How they're sorted
    RecipeName  string         // Which recipe was applied
}
```

### 2. BoardState
```go
type BoardSnapshot struct {
    Columns []BoardColumn
}

type BoardColumn struct {
    Title    string
    Status   model.Status
    Cards    []BoardCard
    
    // Pre-computed stats
    TotalCount  int
    HighPriCount int
    BlockedCount int
}

type BoardCard struct {
    Issue       *model.Issue
    BorderColor lipgloss.Color  // Pre-computed based on status
    Expanded    bool
}
```

### 3. TreeNodes
```go
type TreeNodeSnapshot struct {
    Issue    *model.Issue
    Children []*TreeNodeSnapshot
    Depth    int
    IsLast   bool  // For drawing └── vs ├──
}
```

### 4. GraphLayout
```go
type GraphSnapshot struct {
    Nodes []GraphNode
    Edges []GraphEdge
    Bounds Rect
}

type GraphNode struct {
    Issue *model.Issue
    X, Y  int  // Position in canvas coordinates
}

type GraphEdge struct {
    From, To string
    Path     []Point  // Pre-computed routing
}
```

### 5. InsightsData
```go
type InsightsSnapshot struct {
    Bottlenecks []RankedIssue  // Top 10 by betweenness
    Keystones   []RankedIssue  // Top 10 by critical path
    Influencers []RankedIssue  // Top 10 by eigenvector
    Hubs        []RankedIssue  // Top 10 by HITS hub score
    Authorities []RankedIssue  // Top 10 by HITS authority
    Cycles      [][]string     // All detected cycles
    
    // Pre-rendered strings for quick display
    BottleneckLines []string
    KeystoneLines   []string
    // ...
}
```

## Implementation Location

Extend buildSnapshot() in background_worker.go:

```go
func (w *BackgroundWorker) buildSnapshot() (*DataSnapshot, error) {
    // ... existing loading and analysis code ...
    
    // === Pre-compute view data ===
    
    // 1. List items
    listItems := w.buildListItems(issues, issueMap, currentRecipe)
    
    // 2. Board state
    boardState := w.buildBoardState(issues, issueMap)
    
    // 3. Tree nodes
    treeNodes := w.buildTreeNodes(issues, issueMap)
    
    // 4. Graph layout (can be expensive, might defer)
    graphLayout := w.buildGraphLayout(issues, stats)
    
    // 5. Insights (depends on Phase 2, so might be partial)
    insightsData := w.buildInsightsData(stats)
    
    snapshot := &DataSnapshot{
        // ... existing fields ...
        ListItems:    listItems,
        BoardState:   boardState,
        TreeNodes:    treeNodes,
        GraphLayout:  graphLayout,
        InsightsData: insightsData,
    }
    
    return snapshot, nil
}
```

## Recipe/Filter Integration

Pre-computed views need to respect the current recipe (filters, sorts):

```go
// Worker needs to know current recipe
type BackgroundWorker struct {
    // ...
    currentRecipe atomic.Value  // *recipe.Recipe
}

// UI tells worker about recipe changes
func (w *BackgroundWorker) SetRecipe(r *recipe.Recipe) {
    w.currentRecipe.Store(r)
    w.TriggerRefresh() // Rebuild with new recipe
}
```

## Performance Targets

| View | Current (UI thread) | Target (background) |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|
| List items | 30-50ms | 0ms (pre-computed) |
| Board state | 20-40ms | 0ms (pre-computed) |
| Tree nodes | 20-30ms | 0ms (pre-computed) |
| Graph layout | 50-100ms | 0ms (pre-computed) |
| Insights | 30-50ms | 0ms (pre-computed) |
| **Total** | **150-270ms** | **< 5ms** |

## Subtasks

1. Pre-compute ListItems in background
2. Pre-compute BoardState in background
3. Pre-compute TreeNodes in background
4. Pre-compute GraphLayout in background (or lazy)
5. Pre-compute InsightsData in background
6. Wire recipe changes to trigger refresh

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All view data pre-computed in snapshot
- [ ] View() methods just render pre-computed data
- [ ] Recipe changes trigger snapshot rebuild
- [ ] No noticeable latency in view rendering
- [ ] View transitions remain smooth","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-06T13:36:32.266211094-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-06T13:36:32.266211094-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bv-2h40","depends_on_id":"bv-e3ub","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-06T13:36:45.411407452-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bv-2h40","depends_on_id":"bv-c9xq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-06T13:36:50.562084193-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

### 1. Snapshot Swap Latency

The critical metric: how long does it take to swap snapshots in Update()?

```go
// pkg/ui/benchmark_test.go

func BenchmarkSnapshotSwap(b *testing.B) {
    // Create model with existing snapshot
    model := createTestModel(1000) // 1000 issues
    
    // Pre-create new snapshot
    newSnapshot := createTestSnapshot(1000)
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        msg := SnapshotReadyMsg{Snapshot: newSnapshot}
        model, _ = model.Update(msg)
    }
}

// Expected: < 1μs (just pointer swap)
```

### 2. buildSnapshot() Total Time

How long to build a complete snapshot in background?

```go
func BenchmarkBuildSnapshot(b *testing.B) {
    worker := createTestWorker(b)
    
    for _, size := range []int{100, 500, 1000, 5000} {
        b.Run(fmt.Sprintf("issues=%d", size), func(b *testing.B) {
            setupBeadsFile(b, size)
            
            b.ResetTimer()
            for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
                snapshot, _ := worker.buildSnapshot()
                _ = snapshot
            }
        })
    }
}

// Expected:
// 100 issues: < 50ms
// 500 issues: < 150ms
// 1000 issues: < 300ms
// 5000 issues: < 1s
```

### 7. Coalescing Effectiveness

```go
func BenchmarkCoalescing(b *testing.B) {
    worker := createTestWorker(b)
    setupBeadsFile(b, 100)
    
    var snapshotCount atomic.Int64
    worker.SetSnapshotCallback(func(*DataSnapshot) {
        snapshotCount.Add(1)
    })
    
    worker.Start()
    
    // Simulate rapid writes
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        appendIssueToFile(worker.beadsPath)
        time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)
    }
    
    worker.Stop()
    
    b.ReportMetric(float64(snapshotCount.Load())/float64(b.N), "snapshots/write")
    // Target: << 1.0 (many writes coalesced into few snapshots)
}
```

### 8. End-to-End Latency

```go
func BenchmarkKeyPressLatency(b *testing.B) {
    model := createTestModel(1000)
    
    // Warm up
    model, _ = model.Update(tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyDown})
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        start := time.Now()
        model, _ = model.Update(tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyDown})
        _ = model.View() // Include render time
        latency := time.Since(start)
        
        if latency > 50*time.Millisecond {
            b.Fatalf("latency %v exceeds 50ms target", latency)
        }
    }
}
```

## Background & Motivation
The analysis package computes rich metrics but they're only visible in the insights panel. Users and agents want actionable recommendations: 'These 5 items should be prioritized because they unblock the most work.'

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: 'Smart suggestions' that explain why items should be reprioritized
- For AI Agents: --robot-priority provides a ranked work queue with reasoning

## Technical Approach
1. Create priority scoring function combining multiple metrics
2. Generate recommendations comparing current vs suggested priority
3. CLI: --robot-priority outputs priority-ranked JSON with explanations
4. TUI: 'Suggested' badge/indicator on items

## Scoring Function
impact_score = (
    0.3 * normalized_pagerank +
    0.3 * normalized_betweenness +
    0.2 * blocker_count / max_blocker_count +
    0.1 * freshness_penalty +
    0.1 * explicit_priority_boost
)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-11-26T23:36:10.969534571Z","updated_at":"2025-11-27T01:03:35.76590347Z","closed_at":"2025-11-27T01:03:35.76590347Z","labels":["ai-agent","analysis","prioritization"]}
-{"id":"bv-lkk.1","title":"Implement priority scoring function","description":"Create composite scoring combining PageRank (30%), Betweenness (30%), Blocker count (20%), Staleness (10%), Explicit priority (10%).

## Algorithm
func (a *Analyzer) ComputeImpactScore(issue *model.Issue) ImpactScore:
  - Normalize all metrics to 0-1 range
  - Apply weights: 0.3*pagerank + 0.3*betweenness + 0.2*blockerRatio + 0.1*staleness + 0.1*priorityBoost
  - Return: IssueID, ImpactScore, ScoreBreakdown map

## Staleness Penalty
- Days since update, normalized: min(daysSinceUpdate / 30, 1.0)
- Older items get slight penalty to surface them

## Priority Boost
- P0=1.0, P1=0.75, P2=0.5, P3=0.25, P4=0.0 (higher priority = higher boost)

- pkg/analysis/priority.go: New scoring logic
- pkg/analysis/priority_test.go: Tests with known values","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-26T23:39:56.597660662Z","updated_at":"2025-11-27T00:26:30.580840974Z","closed_at":"2025-11-27T00:26:30.580840974Z","labels":["analysis","core-logic"]}

 {"id":"bv-lnc4","title":"Viewport: Track offset and implement cursor-follows-viewport","description":"## Task: Track Viewport Offset and Cursor-Follows-Viewport

### Background

When the cursor moves, the viewport may need to scroll to keep the cursor visible. This task implements the logic to:
1. Track the current viewport offset (first visible node index)
2. Adjust offset when cursor moves outside visible range
3. Handle scroll behavior preferences

### Implementation

Add field and methods to TreeModel:

```go
type TreeModel struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    viewportOffset int // Index of first visible node
}

// ensureCursorVisible adjusts viewportOffset so cursor is visible.
// Call this after any cursor movement.
func (t *TreeModel) ensureCursorVisible() {
    if len(t.flatList) == 0 {
        return
    }
    
    visibleCount := t.height
    if visibleCount <= 0 {
        visibleCount = 20
    }
    
    // Cursor above viewport - scroll up
    if t.cursor < t.viewportOffset {
        t.viewportOffset = t.cursor
    }
    
    // Cursor below viewport - scroll down
    if t.cursor >= t.viewportOffset + visibleCount {
        t.viewportOffset = t.cursor - visibleCount + 1
    }
    
    // Clamp offset
    maxOffset := len(t.flatList) - visibleCount
    if maxOffset < 0 {
        maxOffset = 0
    }
    if t.viewportOffset > maxOffset {
        t.viewportOffset = maxOffset
    }
    if t.viewportOffset < 0 {
        t.viewportOffset = 0
    }
}
```

### Update navigation methods

Each navigation method should call `ensureCursorVisible()`:

```go
func (t *TreeModel) MoveDown() {
    if t.cursor < len(t.flatList)-1 {
        t.cursor++
        t.ensureCursorVisible()
    }
}

func (t *TreeModel) MoveUp() {
    if t.cursor > 0 {
        t.cursor--
        t.ensureCursorVisible()
    }
}

func (t *TreeModel) PageDown() {
    pageSize := t.height / 2
    if pageSize < 1 {
        pageSize = 5
    }
    t.cursor += pageSize
    if t.cursor >= len(t.flatList) {
        t.cursor = len(t.flatList) - 1
    }
    t.ensureCursorVisible()
}
// ... similar for PageUp, JumpToTop, JumpToBottom, etc.
```

### Scroll behavior options (future consideration)

Could add config for different behaviors:
- **Cursor at edge**: Scroll when cursor hits edge (default)
- **Cursor centered**: Always keep cursor in center
- **Smooth scroll**: Scroll by 3 lines when cursor hits edge

For now, implement cursor-at-edge as it's most intuitive.

### Test cases

```go
func TestEnsureCursorVisible(t *testing.T) {
    tree := createTreeWithNodes(100)
    tree.SetSize(80, 10) // 10 visible lines
    
    // Cursor at 0, offset should be 0
    tree.cursor = 0
    tree.ensureCursorVisible()
    assert(t, tree.viewportOffset == 0)
    
    // Move cursor to 15, offset should adjust
    tree.cursor = 15
    tree.ensureCursorVisible()
    assert(t, tree.viewportOffset == 6) // cursor at bottom of viewport
    
    // Move cursor to 5, offset should scroll back
    tree.cursor = 5
    tree.ensureCursorVisible()
    assert(t, tree.viewportOffset == 5) // cursor at top of viewport
}
```

### Files to modify
- `pkg/ui/tree.go` - Add viewportOffset field, ensureCursorVisible(), update navigation

### Success Criteria
- [ ] Cursor always visible after any navigation
- [ ] Smooth scrolling experience (no jarring jumps)
- [ ] All navigation methods call ensureCursorVisible()
- [ ] Tests cover all edge cases

### Dependencies
- bv-r4ng (visible range calculation)

### Notes
- The `rebuildFlatList()` method should reset viewportOffset or preserve intelligently
- Consider what happens when expand/collapse changes visible nodes","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-05T19:50:36.645079-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-01-05T21:00:48.837014-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-05T21:00:48.837014-05:00","close_reason":"All navigation methods now call ensureCursorVisible().  {"id":"bv-tvp8","title":"AGENTS.md File Detection Logic","description":"# AGENTS.md File Detection Logic

## Background
Detect AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md files in the working directory and check if they already contain our blurb.

## Detection Algorithm

\`\`\`go
type agentFileDetection struct {
    FilePath     string  // Path to found file (empty if none)
    FileType     string  // "AGENTS.md" or "CLAUDE.md"
    HasBlurb     bool    // Already contains our blurb
    BlurbVersion int     // Version of blurb found (0 if none)
}

func detectAgentFile(workDir string) agentFileDetection {
    // Check AGENTS.md first (preferred)
    agentsPath := filepath.Join(workDir, "AGENTS.md")
    if exists(agentsPath) {
        hasBlurb, version := checkForBlurb(agentsPath)
        return agentFileDetection{
            FilePath:     agentsPath,
            FileType:     "AGENTS.md",
            HasBlurb:     hasBlurb,
            BlurbVersion: version,
        }
    }
    
    // Fall back to CLAUDE.md
    claudePath := filepath.Join(workDir, "CLAUDE.md")
    if exists(claudePath) {
        hasBlurb, version := checkForBlurb(claudePath)
        return agentFileDetection{
            FilePath:     claudePath,
            FileType:     "CLAUDE.md",
            HasBlurb:     hasBlurb,
            BlurbVersion: version,
        }
    }
    
    // Neither file exists
    return agentFileDetection{}
}
\`\`\`

## Blurb Marker Detection

\`\`\`go
const blurbMarkerStart = "<!-- bv-agent-instructions-v"
const blurbMarkerEnd   = "-->"

func checkForBlurb(filePath string) (bool, int) {
    content, err := os.ReadFile(filePath)
    if err != nil {
        return false, 0
    }
    
    // Look for marker
    contentStr := string(content)
    idx := strings.Index(contentStr, blurbMarkerStart)
    if idx == -1 {
        return false, 0
    }
    
    // Extract version number
    // e.g., "<!-- bv-agent-instructions-v1 -->"
    // ... parse version ...
    
    return true, version
}
\`\`\`

- File exists but not readable → log warning, skip
- Both files exist → prefer AGENTS.md
- File has partial/corrupted marker → treat as no blurb

 {"id":"bv-tvti","title":"Session-Bead Correlation Engine","description":"# Session-Bead Correlation Engine

## Purpose
The "brain" of the integration—intelligently match cass sessions to beads using multiple strategies. This determines which sessions are relevant to which issues.

## Background

### The Correlation Challenge
Sessions and beads are created independently:
- Users do not explicitly tag sessions with bead IDs
- Session titles may not match bead titles exactly
- Timestamps may not align perfectly
- Same session might relate to multiple beads

We need smart heuristics to find meaningful connections.

### Correlation Quality Matters
False positives (irrelevant sessions shown) erode trust. False negatives (missing relevant sessions) reduce value. We optimize for **precision over recall**—better to show fewer, highly relevant sessions than many questionable ones.

## Correlation Strategies

### Strategy 1: ID Mention (Highest Confidence)
Search for the bead ID literally in sessions:
```go
query := fmt.Sprintf("%q", bead.ID) // e.g., "bv-abc123"
```
**Score bonus**: +100 (definitive match)
**When useful**: When users or agents explicitly mention issue IDs

### Strategy 2: Keyword Extraction (Medium Confidence)
Extract meaningful words from bead title, search with time filter:
```go
keywords := extractKeywords(bead.Title)
// "Fix token refresh timeout" → ["token", "refresh", "timeout"]
query := strings.Join(keywords[:3], " ")
```
**Score bonus**: +30 to +60 (depends on match quality)
**When useful**: Most common case—thematic matching

### Strategy 3: Timestamp Proximity (Lower Confidence)
Sessions occurring near bead activity (creation, updates, closure):
```go
days := daysBetween(bead.CreatedAt, bead.ClosedAt)
if days == 0 { days = 1 }
opts.Days = days + 1 // Search window
```
**Score bonus**: +10 to +30 (time proximity weighting)
**When useful**: Closed beads where work was recent

### Strategy 4: Combined Scoring
Blend strategies for final ranking:
```go
finalScore := baseScore * (1 + timeDecay) * workspaceBoost
// timeDecay: Recent sessions score higher
// workspaceBoost: Same workspace scores 2x
```

## Implementation

### Correlation Hint Type
```go
type CorrelationHint struct {
    BeadID       string          // Which bead this is for
    SessionCount int             // Total matching sessions
    TopSessions  []SearchResult  // Up to 3 best matches
    TopScore     float64         // Highest session score
    Reason       string          // "id_mention", "keywords", "timestamp"
    Keywords     []string        // Keywords used (for display)
}
```

### Correlation Flow
```
For each bead:
1. Check cache first (return if fresh hit)
2. Try ID mention search
   └─ Found → return with high confidence
3. Extract keywords from title
4. Search keywords with time filter
5. Filter by workspace (if available)
6. Score and rank results
7. Cache and return top matches
```

### Keyword Extraction
```go
func extractKeywords(title string) []string {
    // Remove common words: "the", "a", "and", "fix", "add", "update", etc.
    // Remove short words (< 3 chars)
    // Lowercase and dedupe
    // Return up to 5 keywords
}

var stopWords = map[string]bool{
    "the": true, "a": true, "an": true, "and": true, "or": true,
    "fix": true, "add": true, "update": true, "remove": true,
    "implement": true, "create": true, "delete": true,
    "for": true, "to": true, "in": true, "on": true, "at": true,
    "bug": true, "issue": true, "feature": true, "task": true,
}
```

### Workspace Filtering
Critical for relevance—sessions from unrelated projects pollute results:
```go
func workspaceFromBeadsPath(beadsPath string) string {
    // /path/to/project/.beads/beads.jsonl → /path/to/project
    return filepath.Dir(filepath.Dir(beadsPath))
}
```

## Scoring Configuration

### ⚠️ TUNABILITY NOTE
All scoring weights should be **exported constants** that can be adjusted based on real-world usage. Initial values are educated guesses; expect to tune after deployment.

```go
// pkg/cass/scoring.go - All values are tunable
const (
    // Base scores by match type
    ScoreIDMention      = 100  // Definitive evidence
    ScoreExactKeyword   = 50   // Strong thematic link  
    ScorePartialKeyword = 30   // Probable relation
    
    // Multipliers
    MultiplierSameWorkspace = 2.0  // Critical for relevance
    
    // Time decay bonuses
    BonusRecent24h = 20   // Session within 24 hours of bead activity
    BonusRecent7d  = 10   // Session within 7 days
    PenaltyOld30d  = -10  // Session older than 30 days
    
    // Threshold for display
    MinScoreThreshold = 25  // Below this, don't show session
    
    // Result limits
    MaxSessionsReturned = 3  // Top N sessions per bead
    MaxKeywordsExtracted = 5 // Keywords from title
)
```

### Scoring Table (Reference)
| Factor | Weight | Rationale |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| ID mention | +100 | Definitive evidence |
| Exact keyword match | +50 | Strong thematic link |
| Partial keyword match | +30 | Probable relation |
| Same workspace | 2x multiplier | Critical for relevance |
| Recent session (<24h) | +20 | Fresh context |
| Recent session (<7d) | +10 | Relevant context |
| Older session (>30d) | -10 | May be stale |

**Minimum threshold for display: 25 points** (avoids noise)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ID mention strategy works
- [ ] Keyword extraction handles edge cases (empty, very long titles)
- [ ] Timestamp proximity filtering works
- [ ] Combined scoring produces sensible rankings
- [ ] Workspace filtering dramatically improves relevance
- [ ] Results are cached with appropriate TTL
- [ ] Empty beads (no title) handled gracefully
- [ ] All scoring constants are configurable

## Edge Cases
- Bead with empty title → skip keyword strategy, rely on ID/timestamp
- Bead with very long title → truncate to 5 keywords
- Many beads with similar keywords → ensure distinct sessions per bead
- Closed bead with no activity dates → use created_at only
- Workspace path unavailable → search all (lower precision, warn in debug log)
- Title is just "Bug" or "Fix" → all keywords filtered, skip keyword strategy

## Testing Strategy
- Test each strategy in isolation
- Test combined scoring with known inputs/outputs
- Test keyword extraction edge cases
- Test workspace filtering
- Test scoring threshold behavior
- Benchmark performance (target <500ms for single correlation)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-17T15:48:48.016772-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T18:34:57.039754-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T18:34:57.039754-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented Session-Bead Correlation Engine with:

-{"id":"bv-ufd","title":"Context Packs & Recipes System","description":"Implement named workflow presets ('recipes') that bundle filters, layouts, and exports into one-command operations. This dramatically simplifies common workflows for both humans and AI agents.

## Background & Motivation
Users repeatedly perform the same sequences: 'Show me blocked high-priority items' or 'Export a release changelog'. Currently this requires remembering multiple flags. Recipes encode these patterns.

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: 'bv --recipe triage' replaces 'bv --status open,blocked --priority 0,1 --sort priority'
- For AI Agents: Recipes provide semantic entry points - 'triage mode' vs 'release mode' vs 'dependency analysis mode'

## Technical Approach
1. Define recipe schema (YAML)
2. Embed sensible default recipes
3. Allow user-defined recipes in .bv/recipes.yaml
4. CLI: bv --recipe <name> or bv -r <name>
5. TUI: Recipe picker with descriptions

## Built-in Recipes
- triage: Open/blocked issues, sorted by priority
- release-cut: Recently closed issues for changelog
- blocked-review: All blocked items with blocker info
- dependency-risk: High betweenness/PageRank items
- quick-wins: Low-priority, no-dependency items
- stale: Items not updated in 14+ days","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-11-26T23:35:57.447095883Z","updated_at":"2025-11-27T01:15:09.04831944Z","closed_at":"2025-11-27T01:15:09.04831944Z","labels":["ai-agent","developer-experience","workflow"]}

## Schema Fields

-{"id":"bv-ufd.2","title":"Implement recipe loader with embedded defaults","description":"Load recipes from embedded defaults and user configuration, with proper merging.

## Loading Order (later overrides earlier)
1. Embedded defaults (compiled into binary)
2. User config at ~/.config/bv/recipes.yaml (global)
3. Project config at .bv/recipes.yaml (local)

## Embedded Defaults
Use Go embed to include default recipes:
//go:embed defaults/recipes.yaml
var defaultRecipes []byte

## Merge Strategy
- User recipes with same name override defaults completely
- User can disable a default recipe: triage: null
- Unknown keys in user config generate warnings

## Discovery Endpoint
func (l *Loader) ListRecipes() []RecipeSummary
Enables --robot-recipes for AI agent discovery.
